alternatives.transcript: So thanks for giving me a chance to talk about this work. And also thanks to the Institute for bringing me here now for my second year and I was here back in 10/11. And at that time Tom and I began talking with David Hughes about this problem of many body localization. We got sort of started and it got we got it kinda kept bugging us and bugging us and we're working working and it took a return visit, return year. And, but, at this point we've got some results to report. And, so that's what I'm here today to talk about. Just a little bit of background. Fortunately, David Hughes spoke here a couple of weeks ago and gave a lot of background about what many- body localization is. But let me just give you a brief overview summary. I mean you have there's an established field of one body localization, ordinary localization for the Schrodinger operator with a random potential. There are many results on that, rigorous results going back to Frolic and Spencer in the early eighties. But then when it comes to understanding interactions between particles and to what extent the localization phenomena, which is really can be thought of as lack of transport or decay of the eigenfunctions in space. When you get to interacting systems, there's many fewer results available. If you consider a few particles, a handful of particles or multi particles, I guess you could call that, then there was work of Czuliavsky and Suhof and Eismann and Warsaw about five years ago or so, where you consider, let's say, five particles interacting with a random potential and with each other. And then once again it's possible to show that there's no transport and the eigenfunctions decay, that these particles tend to sit in these local potentials, these local spots defined by their external potential. Of course, there's been a lot of physics literature on the blossom really in the last five or so years on the notion of many body localization. And although as David explained, the concept goes all the way back to Anderson's original paper in 'fifty eight. But then the whole field shifted and focused primarily on the one body problem. And then Altshuler, Helliner, and Basco really started to focus a lot on this, idea of localization, sort of so to speak, in fox space rather than in physical space. And then the last few years, Hughes and his collaborators, Powell and others, have, done numerics and, really investigated this whole phenomenon to considerable really develop it considerably. But mathematically, there hasn't been too much to report. There was this recent preprint of discard and et al on the many body localization, although what they needed to do was to take the randomness and jack it up in a system size dependent fashion, I guess exponentially in the system size. So what kind of turns the many body, this large two to the n dimensional space, into effectively a low dimensional system when you check up the randomness that high. So that what we're trying to do this is joint work with Tom Spencer is to look at an actual interacting many body problem and to see what we can actually prove on that system without trying to simplify it by means of tuning up the by, say, working the finite box or tuning up the randomness or in some system size dependent fashion. I'd love to be able to report that we've solved the problem completely, but unfortunately I can't. What we can do is prove you have the many body localization phenomenon occurs provided you have an assumption which I would call limited level attraction, which has to do with the statistics of energy levels in a finite system. And so basically we if the energy levels get too clumped together, that's going to cause problems for our method. And so we have to make an assumption that that doesn't happen. Hopefully further work will enable us or others to weaken that assumption or to eliminate it altogether. But that's where we are at the moment. So Let me so now I do also want say I'm going to be working on a spin chain rather than a one dimensional spin chain rather than on a problem of traditional particles interacting and moving around in space. They're not really that different, but most of the physics work in the last few years has been done on spin chains. And so I'm going to focus on that. This model actually was suggested to us. sentences.text: So thanks for giving me a chance to talk about this work.
And also thanks to the Institute for bringing me here now for my second year and I was here back in 10/11.
And at that time Tom and I began talking with David Hughes about this problem of many body localization.
We got sort of started and it got we got it kinda kept bugging us and bugging us and we're working working and it took a return visit, return year.
And, but, at this point we've got some results to report.
And, so that's what I'm here today to talk about.
Just a little bit of background.
Fortunately, David Hughes spoke here a couple of weeks ago and gave a lot of background about what many- body localization is.
But let me just give you a brief overview summary.
I mean you have there's an established field of one body localization, ordinary localization for the Schrodinger operator with a random potential.
There are many results on that, rigorous results going back to Frolic and Spencer in the early eighties.

But then when it comes to understanding interactions between particles and to what extent the localization phenomena, which is really can be thought of as lack of transport or decay of the eigenfunctions in space.
When you get to interacting systems, there's many fewer results available.

If you consider a few particles, a handful of particles or multi particles, I guess you could call that, then there was work of Czuliavsky and Suhof and Eismann and Warsaw about five years ago or so, where you consider, let's say, five particles interacting with a random potential and with each other.

And then once again it's possible to show that there's no transport and the eigenfunctions decay, that these particles tend to sit in these local potentials, these local spots defined by their external potential.
Of course, there's been a lot of physics literature on the blossom really in the last five or so years on the notion of many body localization.
And although as David explained, the concept goes all the way back to Anderson's original paper in 'fifty eight.
But then the whole field shifted and focused primarily on the one body problem.
And then Altshuler, Helliner, and Basco really started to focus a lot on this, idea of localization, sort of so to speak, in fox space rather than in physical space.
And then the last few years, Hughes and his collaborators, Powell and others, have, done numerics and, really investigated this whole phenomenon to considerable really develop it considerably.
But mathematically, there hasn't been too much to report.

There was this recent preprint of discard and et al on the many body localization, although what they needed to do was to take the randomness and jack it up in a system size dependent fashion, I guess exponentially in the system size.
So what kind of turns the many body, this large two to the n dimensional space, into effectively a low dimensional system when you check up the randomness that high.

So that what we're trying to do this is joint work with Tom Spencer is to look at an actual interacting many body problem and to see what we can actually prove on that system without trying to simplify it by means of tuning up the by, say, working the finite box or tuning up the randomness or in some system size dependent fashion.
I'd love to be able to report that we've solved the problem completely, but unfortunately I can't.

What we can do is prove you have the many body localization phenomenon occurs provided you have an assumption which I would call limited level attraction, which has to do with the statistics of energy levels in a finite system.
And so basically we if the energy levels get too clumped together, that's going to cause problems for our method.
And so we have to make an assumption that that doesn't happen.
Hopefully further work will enable us or others to weaken that assumption or to eliminate it altogether.
But that's where we are at the moment.
